pre_reasoning: Canada are deserved favorites (market ~-140, implying ~55% no-vig) with Jonathan David in form and a possible Alphonso Davies return, versus a defensively stubborn but goal-shy South Africa that scored just twice in the group stage. My win/draw/win estimate is close to the market, but both sides are low-scoring (Under 2.5 is heavily favored and combined totals were 2 or fewer goals in most of their matches), which lifts 90-minute draw value at +250 above its no-vig price for a marginal positive-EV play. Edge is thin and Davies' status is uncertain, so I'm staking small rather than backing the chalk at a negative price.
refl_key_factor_missed: In a low-total match the superior side still tends to nick the one decisive goal, so a tight game favors the favorite winning by one as much as it favors a draw.
refl_key_factor_overweighted: The draw probability, inflated by 'low-scoring therefore more draws' reasoning, when the quality gap pointed to a 1-0/2-0 Canada win that the analysts had flagged.
refl_counterfactual: If South Africa had converted one of their counter-attacking chances—their only realistic route to a result—the game could plausibly have ended level or as an upset.
refl_would_bet_differently: yes — my own note said the draw edge was within model noise, so no_bet was the more disciplined call regardless of outcome, since a ~1.5% estimated edge under high lineup uncertainty (Davies' status) wasn't robust enough to stake.

pre_reasoning: Germany are rightly heavy favorites (~-290 / ~70% implied), with elite attackers (Wirtz, Musiala, Sané, Havertz), but the moneyline carries no value at that price and Paraguay at +850 is a true longshot. Paraguay are built as a deep, compact low block that has produced back-to-back clean sheets (Türkiye, Australia), and Germany have looked defensively sloppy and lost their last group game to Ecuador, so the stylistic matchup and 2002 R16 precedent (a 1-0 grind) make a 90-minute draw modestly underpriced at +390. I take a small value stake on the draw while keeping Germany as clear favorites to advance overall.
refl_key_factor_missed: I underweighted how completely Paraguay's deep block could neutralize Germany's superior attack and drag the tie to extra time and a shootout, and I gave no explicit weight to the knockout penalty path where the 'draw' outcome means Paraguay actually advance.
refl_key_factor_overweighted: The Germany talent/firepower gap — I had them at 68% to win in regulation, overrating the likelihood that an elite attack breaks down a purpose-built low block within 90 minutes against a side that had already kept two clean sheets.
refl_counterfactual: If Germany had converted even one of the chances their expected territorial dominance should have produced, they likely win in 90 minutes and the draw/shootout never happens.
refl_would_bet_differently: No — the draw at +390 was the correct value identification given the stylistic mismatch, and it cashed; the only tweak would be also considering a 'Paraguay to advance' line to capture the shootout upside.

pre_reasoning: Models and de-vigged markets agree Netherlands are slight favorites (Opta 47.6/27.4/25.0; bet365 implied ~45/29/26; Kalshi 46/31/25), and my win-market numbers match closely enough that those bets carry no real edge. The draw at plus-money (~+230) is the only modest value angle: Morocco are unbeaten inside 90 minutes in 32 straight, the Netherlands have conceded in every group game with no clean sheet in seven, and multiple previews point to a cagey, low-scoring, level-after-90 knockout between two well-organized sides. Stake kept small because the edge is thin in a coin-flip-ish single match.
refl_key_factor_missed: Morocco's knockout temperament and shootout strength — I had the Dutch favored to progress, but Morocco neutralized them in open play and went through on penalties, a scenario my own sources (RotoWire) flagged and I underweighted in the win probabilities.
refl_key_factor_overweighted: The Netherlands' group-stage attacking output; 10 goals against weaker defenses didn't translate against Morocco's disciplined block, which held them to a single goal over 120 minutes.
refl_counterfactual: If the Dutch had finished one of their open-play chances or converted in the shootout they advance, but the regulation result I actually bet on — a draw — resolves identically.
refl_would_bet_differently: no — the draw was the correct value angle given two organized sides and a likely level-after-90 game, and it landed; the process holds up independent of the shootout, though I'd note Morocco-to-progress was the under-respected side bet.

pre_reasoning: Market lines (Brazil ~-145, Draw ~+270/+290, Japan ~+400/+420) de-vig to roughly Brazil 56% / Draw 25% / Japan 19%, and Brazil's class, form (back-to-back 3-0s) and attacking firepower (Vini Jr 4 goals, Cunha 3) make them deserved favorites. However, Brazil's big wins came against Haiti/Scotland (neither advanced), Raphinha is injured, and Japan are unbeaten in a tougher group with a containment style that produced two 90-min draws (vs Netherlands and Sweden) plus a 3-2 win over Brazil in the 2025 Kirin Cup, so the regulation draw is modestly underpriced at +290. I take a small value stake on the draw rather than the negative-EV favorite, sizing well below full Kelly given the edge is thin and within model error.
refl_key_factor_missed: I underweighted that an open, high-event game (the tournament's Over-2.5 trend, both attacks scoring) makes a 90-minute draw less likely; a 2-1 with Japan scoring but losing is exactly the competitive-favorite-wins scenario my 55% Brazil line implied.
refl_key_factor_overweighted: Japan's draw-prone containment style and the 2025 Kirin Cup friendly win as evidence the gap had narrowed enough to make the regulation draw a value play.
refl_counterfactual: If Japan had converted one more late chance to equalize, it goes to a 90-minute draw and my bet wins, so the result hinged on Brazil's superior finishing in a genuinely close game.
refl_would_bet_differently: yes — the draw edge was only ~5% at the best price and within model error, so the disciplined call was no_bet or a smaller stake rather than chasing a thin contrarian value angle.

pre_reasoning: Mexico are deserved favorites as imperious hosts (9/9 points, three clean sheets) playing in Mexico City, and the no-vig market puts them around 43-45% with Ecuador near 24-25% (+290). However, Mexico's clean sheets came against modest attacks (South Africa, South Korea, Czechia), while Ecuador just came from behind to beat Germany 2-1 and are elite altitude specialists (home in Quito at ~2,850m), which neutralizes Mexico's biggest edge here. I nudge Ecuador slightly above market to ~26%, making +290 a thin positive-EV value play, so I stake a small $10 given the genuine but modest edge and high knockout variance.
refl_key_factor_missed: Mexico's defensive control and squad cohesion were strong enough to produce a comfortable clean-sheet win, not the tense 1-0/extra-time grind I leaned toward; I underweighted how decisively the host could dominate.
refl_key_factor_overweighted: The 'Ecuador beat Germany + altitude specialists' narrative — I inflated Ecuador's win probability above market (0.26) specifically to manufacture a value bet that wasn't really there.
refl_counterfactual: If Ecuador had taken an early chance to lead, the game might have opened up, but given their well-documented poor finishing a different result was unlikely.
refl_would_bet_differently: yes — I'd go no_bet, since my true edge was within estimation noise and the efficient-market read was the correct one.

pre_reasoning: France were perfect in Group I (10 goals scored, 2 conceded; 4-1 over Norway) with a deep, in-form attack and Saliba returning, while Sweden sneaked in as a best third-place team with a dangerous Gyökeres-Isak strike pair but a leaky defense missing Isak Hien — a clear France edge. My ~74% France estimate essentially matches the vig-removed market (France ~-330/-350 ⇒ ~73%), so there is no positive-EV edge on any of the three outcomes. With no value after the bookmaker margin, the disciplined play is to pass rather than bet a near-fair or slightly negative line.
refl_key_factor_missed: I underweighted France's defensive solidity (Saliba back, Upamecano in form) — I expected Sweden to likely score at least once, but France kept a clean sheet and fully neutralized Gyökeres and Isak.
refl_key_factor_overweighted: Sweden's strike-pairing threat; I treated Gyökeres-Isak as likely to make the game open, but they never got the platform to hurt France.
refl_counterfactual: If Sweden had converted an early chance to open the game up, it would have been more competitive, but the quality gap means France still most likely wins.
refl_would_bet_differently: no, one comfortable win doesn't retroactively create value at -330 — passing on a no-edge line was still the disciplined call regardless of the outcome.

pre_reasoning: Analysts unanimously frame this as a near coin-flip between two sides separated by only a couple of FIFA ranking places, yet the market prices Norway clearly ahead (~1/1) largely on Haaland's four group-stage goals, leaving Côte d'Ivoire generously priced at ~11/4. The Elephants are well-organized, hard to break down, beat France in a pre-tournament friendly, and face a Norway defense that conceded seven goals in the group stage—so the underdog price offers slim value, though injury doubts (Singo, Diallo) and CIV's shaky finishing temper conviction. This is a low-conviction value lean rather than a strong edge, hence the small stake.
refl_key_factor_missed: Norway's top-end quality (Haaland/Odegaard) as the decisive edge—I let the 'coin flip' narrative pull Côte d'Ivoire above the vig-removed market number when the market's Norway lean was probably right.
refl_key_factor_overweighted: The pundit 'coin flip' framing plus CIV's friendly win over France, which I used to manufacture underdog value and justify betting against my own modal outcome.
refl_counterfactual: It was a one-goal game, so had Ivory Coast converted more of their chances—their known finishing weakness—or held on for a draw into extra time, the outcome could easily have flipped.
refl_would_bet_differently: yes—my own Kelly check showed the edge was essentially noise (~$0.45 full Kelly), so staking $15 on the underdog against my own most-likely outcome was undisciplined; no_bet was the correct call.

pre_reasoning: USA are clear home favorites (FIFA #15 vs #61, in-form after winning Group D, rested starters like Adams/Balogun/Pulisic returning, home Bay Area crowd), so ~68% in regulation. Market prices USA at -280 (negative EV after vig) and Bosnia at +800; my ~12% estimate slightly exceeds the ~10.5% de-vigged market given single-game knockout variance, USA's leaky defense (one clean sheet in 11) plus a four-match knockout losing streak, and Bosnia's real attacking threat (Dzeko, Demirovic, Alajbegovic) after upsetting Italy to qualify. Only a thin value edge exists, so the stake is small and high-variance.
refl_key_factor_missed: I underweighted how decisively Bosnia's chronically leaky defense would let USA's strong, rested attack control the game, making a comfortable USA win more likely than my 12% Bosnia figure implied.
refl_key_factor_overweighted: USA's 'four straight knockout losses' jinx and their defensive frailty—narrative factors I used to nudge Bosnia above the market's ~10.5%, none of which materialized.
refl_counterfactual: If Bosnia had scored first and forced USA to chase, the leaky-USA-defense scenario I bet on could have opened up, but with USA's first-choice XI at home nothing suggests the overall result would have flipped.
refl_would_bet_differently: yes—I would pick no_bet or a far smaller stake, since the Bosnia edge was razor-thin, narrative-driven, and my $10 stake was aggressive relative to what Kelly (~$1) justified.

pre_reasoning: De-vigged consensus market odds (BEL ~+115, Draw ~+220, SEN ~+260) imply roughly 44/30/26, and this is widely framed as a coin-flip dressed up as a slight Belgium edge; Belgium looked toothless against organized defenses (1-1 Egypt, 0-0 Iran) before feasting on weak New Zealand, and Senegal will sit compact and counter through Mane/Sarr's pace. Multiple sharp handicappers converge on the draw as the value play in an evenly matched, likely tight-in-regulation knockout, so a small plus-money stake on the draw carries a marginal edge. The stake is intentionally small because the edge is thin, Senegal's loss of first-choice keeper Mendy is a real downgrade favoring Belgium, and Kelly sizing on a near-efficient market is minimal.
refl_key_factor_missed: I underweighted that Belgium's superior attacking quality (De Bruyne/Lukaku/Trossard) would actually convert in an open, end-to-end game rather than the match staying tight and low-scoring.
refl_key_factor_overweighted: The sharp-handicapper 'tense, low-scoring regulation draw' narrative and Belgium's toothlessness vs organized defenses—Senegal played open and countered rather than parking the bus, giving Belgium's attack the space it lacked vs Egypt/Iran.
refl_counterfactual: If Senegal had first-choice keeper Mendy instead of backup Diaw (who shipped 3), or if the game had stayed as tight as I bet on, one fewer Belgium goal likely makes it 2-2.
refl_would_bet_differently: yes—my own Kelly math already pointed to a near-zero stake, so no_bet or ~$5 would have been the internally consistent call rather than $20 on a 31% outcome.

pre_reasoning: Market (~-380) and Opta's supercomputer (73.9% England in regulation) both peg England near 74-76%, so backing the favorite offers no value after the vig. The one soft spot is the draw: England have repeatedly labored against deep low blocks (Ghana stalemate, grind vs Panama) and DR Congo are precisely that opponent—organized, resilient, counter-threat via Wissa—having already held Portugal 1-1. At +440 (~18.5% implied) I estimate the true 90-minute draw chance slightly higher (~19%), a thin value edge, so a small stake rather than a conviction bet.
refl_key_factor_missed: England's individual quality (Kane, Bellingham, Saka) still producing the decisive goals to break the block—the favorite converting despite the grind, rather than the game staying level.
refl_key_factor_overweighted: The 'England struggle against low blocks, therefore elevated draw chance' narrative, which pushed me into a bet against a well-priced favorite on a razor-thin edge.
refl_counterfactual: If DR Congo had converted one more counter or set piece and England hadn't found a second goal, the low-block thesis could have held for a draw—but England's talent decided it.
refl_would_bet_differently: yes, I would have chosen no_bet since the de-vigged edge on the draw (~0.5-1%) was too thin to justify betting against a sharply priced favorite, even though my probability forecast itself was sound.

pre_reasoning: Switzerland won Group B unbeaten (7 goals scored, 3 conceded) on a 9-match unbeaten run with midfield control via Xhaka/Freuler and no major injuries, while Algeria backed into R32 as a third-place team having conceded 7 goals in 3 games, with key forward Amoura a major doubt and goalkeeper uncertainty (Zidane/Benbot). Market and prediction platforms price a Swiss 90-min win near 50-54% and my estimate leans slightly higher given the fresh Algeria injury/keeper news, so I back Switzerland but keep the stake modest since the edge over the price is thin and single-game knockout variance (plus Mahrez's threat) is real.
refl_key_factor_missed: I gave Algeria too much credit for scoring (they'd scored in all 3 group games), but their injury-hit attack was fully shut out — Switzerland's defense was better than the 'conceded in every group game' narrative implied.
refl_key_factor_overweighted: Knockout-tightening draw risk and Algeria's counter-attacking threat via Mahrez, which never materialized in what was a fairly controlled Swiss win.
refl_counterfactual: Had Amoura been fully fit and Algeria scored an early goal, the tie could have been genuinely tight, but Switzerland's midfield control still made them the likely progressor.
refl_would_bet_differently: no, the pick was right and the modest $35 stake was appropriate given a thin edge over an efficient market and real single-game variance.

pre_reasoning: De-vigged market odds imply roughly Portugal 54% / draw 27% / Croatia 19%, and I broadly agree—Portugal have deeper, more talented attackers versus an aging Croatia side that shipped four to England. My only lean is that a regulation draw is marginally underpriced: Portugal were held to 0-0 by Colombia and 1-1 by DR Congo, and Croatia's Modric-Kovacic core is built to slow the game and grind knockouts, so the best available draw price (~+275) offers a thin positive-EV edge worth a small stake. This is a marginal-value play, not a confident one—hence the modest $10 rather than backing a short-priced favorite with no edge.
refl_key_factor_missed: In a win-or-go-home knockout, Portugal's raw talent edge was more likely to assert itself than in low-stakes group games; I underweighted how knockout urgency plus Croatia's England-exposed defensive frailty would let Portugal's attack convert.
refl_key_factor_overweighted: Portugal's 'frustration-prone' group form (0-0 with Colombia, 1-1 with DR Congo) as evidence they'd be held again, and Croatia's aging-core 'tempo control' as a draw driver.
refl_counterfactual: It finished 2-1, so a single late Croatia equalizer would have produced the regulation draw and cashed my bet—the outcome hinged on one goal.
refl_would_bet_differently: yes—the draw edge rested on an overextended narrative and was within estimation noise, so no_bet was the more disciplined call.

pre_reasoning: Spain are deserved heavy favorites—three group-stage clean sheets, superior squad depth and Yamal—and my 90-minute estimate (Spain 71% / draw 18% / Austria 11%) closely tracks both Opta's supercomputer (70.6/17.3/12.2) and de-vigged FanDuel lines. That makes Spain's -300 moneyline slightly negative-EV after vig (breakeven ~75% vs my 71%), and the draw is roughly fair, so there is no edge backing the favorite. The only marginal value is Austria at +900 (implied ~10%), a hair below my 11% and Opta's 12.2%, so I stake a small speculative amount on the underdog while acknowledging the edge is thin and within model noise.
refl_key_factor_missed: I underweighted how completely Spain's midfield control would smother Austria's press and expose their leaky defense—the 3-0 margin points to a wider quality gap than my 71% implied.
refl_key_factor_overweighted: I gave too much credence to Austria's 'upset path' (Spain missing wide attackers, Rodri below form, Yamal-dependence), which justified a small value bet on a longshot whose real edge was within model noise.
refl_counterfactual: Even if Austria had converted an early press turnover, Spain's control and squad depth likely still produce a win, so the result looks robust rather than scoreline-fragile.
refl_would_bet_differently: yes—I'd pass (no_bet), since the ~1-point Austria edge over the implied price was illusory and the disciplined move was to avoid a -EV-after-vig favorite and a noise-level longshot alike.

pre_reasoning: Colombia are clear favorites—unbeaten Group K winners (7 pts, conceded once) with superior individuals (Díaz, Rodríguez, Muñoz) and no injuries; Opta simulations and the market both land ~64-65% for Colombia, matching my read, so there's no edge backing the favorite at -188. Ghana are blunt (2 goals, 2.86 xG in the group) but Queiroz's disciplined low block frustrated England, and Colombia's habit of grinding out tight 1-0s (24 shots, 0 goals vs Portugal) keeps upset/penalty variance live. Ghana at ~+700 is the only positive-EV option under my 14% estimate versus the ~12.5% implied, so a small longshot value stake is the disciplined play.
refl_key_factor_missed: I underweighted how completely Ghana's attacking impotence (2 goals all tournament) capped their realistic win chance—my 14% was likely a touch generous versus the market's ~12.5%.
refl_key_factor_overweighted: Knockout upset variance and Colombia's trouble breaking down low blocks, which I used to justify the Ghana longshot even though Colombia still ground out their trademark 1-0.
refl_counterfactual: If Ghana had manufactured one clean counter-attacking chance for Semenyo or Sulemana it might have forced a draw, but given their tournament-long attacking drought that scenario was always improbable.
refl_would_bet_differently: yes—no_bet, because both the market and Opta matched my own read, so my thin Ghana edge was within noise and didn't justify staking on a longshot.

pre_reasoning: Argentina are reigning champions in excellent form (three group wins, eight goals, Messi leading scorers with six) versus debutants Cape Verde, who reached the Round of 32 on defensive resilience but minimal attacking output and now face a clear step up in opponent quality. Market consensus (Kalshi ~87%, bet365 -600, Oddsshark implied ~85-95%) closely matches my ~84% estimate, so backing Argentina carries negative EV after vig, while the draw (~12%) and the upset (~4%) offer no clear enough edge to justify a stake. Cape Verde's 0-0 vs Spain shows they can frustrate elite sides, but that value is already priced in, so the disciplined play is to pass.
refl_key_factor_missed: Cape Verde's counter-attacking/transition scoring threat — they had already put two past Uruguay in the group stage, a signal I dismissed by framing their run as purely defensive; scoring twice against Argentina shows I underrated their ability to hurt elite sides.
refl_key_factor_overweighted: The 'one-sided mismatch' narrative and Argentina's defensive control — I assumed the quality gap would yield a comfortable, low-danger win rather than the open 3-2 thriller it became.
refl_counterfactual: If Cape Verde had converted one more chance to make it 3-3, the match would have gone to extra time and the outcome could easily have swung the other way.
refl_would_bet_differently: no — my no-bet on the moneyline was disciplined and the near-upset confirms Argentina at ~-600 offered poor value, though in hindsight a both-teams-to-score or over 2.5 goals bet would have won.

pre_reasoning: Sportsbooks (FanDuel/bet365) price Egypt ~+150, draw ~+190, Australia ~+230, implying de-vigged probabilities near Egypt 38% / draw 33% / Australia 29%; Egypt is a modest favorite on Salah's and Marmoush's individual quality. I nudge Egypt down and the draw up because Salah is managing a hamstring strain (cleared to feature but possibly not starting), Australia's profile is a classic draw-magnet (elite defensive structure, failed to score in two of three group games), and books set a very low goal total (o/u 1.5-2.5) signaling a tight, cautious knockout tie. A small stake on the draw at +190 is the only outcome offering marginal positive expected value against my estimate; the market is otherwise efficient, so the stake is kept conservative.
refl_key_factor_missed: I underweighted Australia's ability to actually score in a knockout — they got on the scoresheet (1-1) despite my 'chronic shortage of attacking threat' framing that implied a 0-0-type grind; it didn't hurt the draw bet, but my goal-suppression read was slightly too strong.
refl_key_factor_overweighted: Salah's hamstring as a dampener on Egypt — I shaded Egypt down for it, yet Egypt still produced enough to draw and then advanced on penalties, so the downgrade was a touch aggressive.
refl_counterfactual: If Australia had held their nerve from the spot (they lost the shootout 4-2), they would have advanced instead of Egypt, but the regulation 1-1 draw — the outcome I forecast and staked on — would have been unchanged.
refl_would_bet_differently: no — the process was sound: I correctly identified a defensively-solid, low-scoring, Salah-compromised tie as more draw-prone than the market priced, and the small-stake draw bet at +190 profited.

pre_reasoning: Team A is Paraguay (W74), Team B is France (W77). France are deserved heavy favorites — four wins, no losses, Mbappé leading the Golden Boot race, and elite attacking depth (Dembélé, Olise, Barcola) behind a solid back line, while Paraguay are a defensively stubborn Cinderella who beat Germany on penalties but carry attacking injury doubts (Enciso, Alderete). My numbers sit essentially on the market/Kalshi consensus (~80% France, ~14% draw, ~6% Paraguay), so France at -500/-600 is clearly negative EV, and the razor-thin edges on the draw or a Paraguay upset shrink to sub-1% Kelly stakes — the disciplined call is to pass.
refl_key_factor_missed: none — if anything, the 1-0 vindicated my sub-market view that Paraguay's low block would suppress France's scoring rather than allow the projected 3-0 blowout.
refl_key_factor_overweighted: none material; Paraguay's attacking-injury doubts (Enciso, Alderete) mattered less than expected since the game hinged on their defense, which held France to a single goal.
refl_counterfactual: The match was one goal from a draw-and-penalties scenario, so had Paraguay converted a counter or defended the decisive goal, the outcome could plausibly have flipped.
refl_would_bet_differently: no — France won but only 1-0, and the -EV France price plus sub-1% Kelly edges on the draw/Paraguay make no_bet process-correct regardless of the result.

pre_reasoning: Team A is Canada (W73, beat South Africa 1-0) and Team B is Morocco (W75, beat Netherlands on penalties). Morocco is the clear side to back — 2022 semifinalists, AFCON champions on a long unbeaten run, deeper and more talented (Hakimi, Diaz, Saibari), and 3-1 all-time vs Canada — while Canada is organized and dangerous through Jonathan David but inconsistent and missing Kone. My estimates land essentially on top of the de-vigged market (Morocco ~53%, draw ~27%, Canada ~19%) and prediction-market prices (Kalshi ~55/27/18), so there is no exploitable edge; the only marginal value is the draw, given both teams' strong low-scoring/under trends and a cautious favorite prone to extra time, but the edge is too thin to stake.
refl_key_factor_missed: Morocco's ceiling — I underweighted the quality/depth gap and their ability to convert dominance into a comfortable multi-goal win, not just a narrow one.
refl_key_factor_overweighted: The 'two organized, low-scoring sides = tight, draw-prone game' thesis, plus Canada's flattering underlying xG numbers, which pulled my draw probability up and my picture of the margin toward a coin-flip grind.
refl_counterfactual: If Canada had taken an early chance to level the game state, it might have stayed competitive longer, but Morocco's clear superiority means they most likely still win.
refl_would_bet_differently: no — no_bet was the disciplined and correct call, and the marginal draw lean I declined to stake would have lost, so restraint was validated.

pre_reasoning: England (FIFA #4) have the superior squad and Kane in historic form (5 goals in 4 games), so the market rightly makes them slight favorites (~+140, implied ~40%). But Mexico enters unbeaten with four clean sheets and, crucially, plays at the Azteca (2,200m altitude, hostile crowd) where they've lost just twice in 89 competitive matches and no World Cup side has beaten them in 10 attempts—an edge markets tend to underprice, especially with England's right side depleted by injuries (James, Quansah out, Rice hampered). At plus-money (~+205/+210), Mexico offers modest value, so I take a small disciplined stake rather than backing a fully-priced favorite.
refl_key_factor_missed: England's individual attacking quality (Kane/Bellingham/wide pace) was decisive enough to score three away at altitude—I underweighted how much elite finishing can neutralize a fortress venue.
refl_key_factor_overweighted: The Azteca altitude/home-crowd 'fortress' effect, which I let push Mexico up and drive the bet even though it wasn't enough to overcome the talent gap.
refl_counterfactual: If Mexico had converted a chance against England's injury-depleted right flank to level late, the 3-2 becomes a draw or 3-3, but England's finishing edge realistically decided it.
refl_would_bet_differently: yes—I backed Mexico on a ~2% thin edge while my own model made England the modal pick, so I should have chosen no_bet or a much smaller stake rather than $25 against my own favorite.

pre_reasoning: The de-vigged market consensus is roughly Brazil 52% / draw 26% / Norway 22%, and Brazil is the higher-quality, better-defended side (0.74 xGA per game), so this is close to a fair line. I lean marginally toward Norway being underpriced because Brazil's midfield is depleted (Paqueta out, Raphinha only bench-fit), their fullbacks are exploitable by Nusa/Odegaard, and Haaland has a strong scoring record versus Brazil's specific center-backs (6 goals in 11 vs Gabriel). Norway to win outright at the best price (+360 ≈ 21.7% implied) offers a thin positive-EV value lean, so I stake a small amount rather than backing the efficient Brazil/draw prices.
refl_key_factor_missed: I underweighted how thoroughly Brazil's depleted midfield (Paqueta out, Raphinha unfit) would fail to control the game, letting Norway's Haaland-Odegaard axis dictate a full win rather than just a scare.
refl_key_factor_overweighted: Brazil's pedigree and defensive record (0.74 xGA/game, clean sheets) — I let their class anchor them at 0.50 despite citing structural fragilities that my own reasoning suggested were more decisive.
refl_counterfactual: If Brazil had a fit, functioning midfield to shield their exploitable fullbacks, the game likely swings back to a Brazil win or draw.
refl_would_bet_differently: no — the Norway value lean was correct on process and the stake was appropriately small for a thin edge, so increasing it based on one favorable result would just be outcome bias.

pre_reasoning: The market is essentially a pick 'em (Opta: USA 37.2% / Belgium 36.5%; no-vig book lines ~36% each, draw ~28%), so there's no large edge in any direction. I nudge slightly toward the USA on home advantage in Seattle, a partisan crowd, strong tournament form (2+ goals in all four games), and Balogun's reinstatement, against a Belgium side that has underwhelmed and needed a late miracle to survive Senegal. The best available USA price (+170, ~37% breakeven) gives a thin positive-EV lean, so I take only a small stake given genuine uncertainty and Belgium's higher individual ceiling and lopsided head-to-head history.
refl_key_factor_missed: Belgium's individual attacking quality and the predictive weight of their recent 5-2 head-to-head win, which I explicitly discounted as 'just friendlies' but which signaled a real talent gap that showed up as a 3-goal margin.
refl_key_factor_overweighted: USA's home-field advantage and partisan Seattle crowd, plus their tournament goal-scoring form, which I let nudge me to the wrong side of a pick-'em market.
refl_counterfactual: Given the decisive 4-1 margin, no single change (crowd, an early goal, one lineup tweak) plausibly flips the result—a more compact USA midblock might have narrowed it but almost certainly not won it.
refl_would_bet_differently: yes—I would have chosen no_bet or a small Belgium lean, respecting the individual-quality and head-to-head signals over the home bump.

pre_reasoning: Market and models converge on Spain as clear favorites (Opta 49.2%, Kalshi 51%, bet365 -111), driven by four clean sheets and a 34-game unbeaten run; my Spain and Portugal numbers essentially match the market, so neither offers value. The one soft edge is the draw at +250 (implied ~28.6%): this Iberian derby has produced repeated draws (3-3 in 2018, 2-2 in 2025, plus 0-0/1-1 meetings), both sides have played cautiously (Portugal 0-0 vs Colombia, Spain 0-0 vs Cape Verde), and Spain's clean sheets came against weaker attacks than Portugal's, so I shade the 90-min draw slightly higher than the market and stake small given the thin edge and high knockout variance.
refl_key_factor_missed: Spain's quality edge means a tight, low-scoring game favors them to nick it 1-0 rather than stay level; I treated 'tight' as 'likely drawn' when tight matches usually break toward the better side.
refl_key_factor_overweighted: The Iberian derby's history of high draw frequency and both teams' cautious tendencies, which led me to shade the draw above the market and stake on it.
refl_counterfactual: If Portugal had converted one of their chances the game likely finishes level, so the single-goal result hinged on fine margins rather than a clear performance gap.
refl_would_bet_differently: yes, I would have chosen no_bet since my draw lean rested on a soft qualitative edge that was quantitatively negligible against an efficient market.

pre_reasoning: The market, Opta (Colombia 41.9% in 90 min), and Kalshi all converge on Colombia as slight favorites (~42%), a draw ~31%, and Switzerland ~27% — an unusually tight, low-scoring knockout between two mean defenses (Colombia conceded 1 in 4; Switzerland on 3 straight wins). I nudge Switzerland up modestly because Colombia has failed to beat any European side in 2026 (D1 L2 vs Croatia/France/Portugal), while Switzerland brings form (unbeaten in 10), an emerging Manzambi, and a third consecutive match at BC Place. At the best-available 11/4 (3.75) that lean creates thin positive EV, so I take a small value stake on Switzerland rather than the vig-laden favorite.
refl_key_factor_missed: My own reasoning flagged a low-scoring, extra-time-bound tie between two elite defenses, yet I underweighted that this pointed to the draw as the outcome most likely to actually hit in regulation (0-0), which it did.
refl_key_factor_overweighted: The thin line-shopping 'value' on Switzerland at 11/4 plus the Colombia-can't-beat-Europeans fade — I let a marginal, self-generated edge push me onto the underdog to win outright, when Colombia (my modeled favorite) advanced.
refl_counterfactual: If either side had converted a single clear chance in regulation the tie avoids penalties and could have produced a decisive result, but as a 0-0 shootout my team_a_win pick was always going to lose regardless of who advanced.
refl_would_bet_differently: yes — consistent with my own tight/low-scoring read I should have backed the draw or chosen no_bet rather than betting Switzerland to win a game I expected to be decided by a coin-flip shootout.

pre_reasoning: Argentina are deserved heavy favorites (Opta 69.1%, market ~70% de-vigged), and their money line (-290) and the draw (+370) both price in a bigger edge than I'd give them, especially after they were nearly upset by Cape Verde and are carrying fatigue/injury concerns (Messi 39 and admittedly tired, no subs left last game, González/Medina doubtful). My Egypt estimate of ~12% (aligned with Opta's 12.3%) sits above the +900 implied ~10%, making Egypt the only positive-EV option, so I take a deliberately small, high-variance longshot stake while passing on the -EV favorite and draw. This is a speculative thin-edge bet, not a confidence play — Argentina remain far more likely to win.
refl_key_factor_missed: I failed to separate Egypt's realistic ceiling (staying competitive and scoring) from what my bet actually needed (an outright win); the 3-2 mirrored the Cape Verde game almost exactly, which my qualitative reasoning had flagged but my bet ignored.
refl_key_factor_overweighted: Argentina's fatigue and defensive vulnerability as a driver of an Egypt victory — those factors translated into Egypt goals, not into Egypt actually winning.
refl_counterfactual: If Egypt had converted one more chance to force 3-3 it would have gone to extra time, but Argentina's superior quality made an outright Egypt win unlikely regardless, so the winner almost certainly does not change.
refl_would_bet_differently: yes — not because the longshot lost (that was the ~88% base case), but because the edge was within my estimate's noise and Egypt's plausible upside was scoring rather than winning, so I'd pass on the money line or express it via a goals/both-teams-to-score market instead.

pre_reasoning: France are the tournament's most complete side (unbeaten, 14-2 goal difference, deep attack led by in-form Mbappé), while Morocco lose top scorer Ismael Saibari to a hamstring injury, blunting their attack; France's minor concern is Tchouaméni's thigh, well covered by Koné/Rabiot. My regulation-time estimates (France ~61% / Draw ~25% / Morocco ~14%) sit right on top of the vig-free market and the Opta (60.9%) and Kalshi (62/24/14) consensus, so I have no informational edge. At FanDuel's -175 / +290 / +550 lines every outcome carries negative expected value after the bookmaker margin, so the disciplined call is to pass.
refl_key_factor_missed: none
refl_key_factor_overweighted: The draw probability (0.25) was likely a touch high; with Saibari out, Morocco's attack was gutted, and the clean sheet suggests a decisive France regulation win was a bit more probable than I allowed.
refl_counterfactual: Had Saibari been fit, Morocco might have generated a goal and made it tenser, but France's overall superiority meant a French win was still the likely result.
refl_would_bet_differently: no, because no_bet reflected a genuine lack of edge against efficient market pricing, and the outcome landing on the favorite does not retroactively make betting into negative EV correct.

pre_reasoning: Spain are clear favorites in this LA quarterfinal: the only side yet to concede at the tournament, reigning European champions, and fully fit, whereas Belgium are potent in attack (De Bruyne, Lukaku, De Ketelaere, Trossard) but defensively leaky (conceded in three straight wins) and have lost midfielder Amadou Onana to a tournament-ending ACL injury. My probabilities sit close to the market (Spain ~-155, Draw +290, Belgium +425 imply ~58/24/18 after removing vig), so there is little pure value, but Spain is my highest-conviction outcome, hence a modest stake rather than a large one. Belgium's ceiling and Spain's tendency to win tight (1-0 vs Portugal) keep meaningful draw/upset probability, so I avoid overcommitting.
refl_key_factor_missed: Belgium's attacking talent breaching Spain's defense — Spain conceded for the first time in the tournament, which I treated as unlikely given their clean-sheet streak.
refl_key_factor_overweighted: Spain's 'yet to concede' record; I leaned on it as a signal of defensive impregnability, but they gave up a goal as I'd half-flagged Belgium could.
refl_counterfactual: If Belgium had converted an additional chance to equalize the 2-1, the match likely goes to extra time and the outcome becomes a coin flip.
refl_would_bet_differently: no — the pick was correct and the conservative stake was appropriate given the short price and the genuinely competitive one-goal margin; betting bigger in hindsight would just be outcome bias.

pre_reasoning: Argentina are rightful favorites — defending champions, Messi leading the Golden Boot with 8 goals, and an 11-match unbeaten World Cup streak — with both the de-vigged market and Opta's supercomputer placing them near 56-57% in regulation. But Argentina have been defensively fragile (conceding from 56% of shots on target faced, needing late 3-2 escapes vs Cape Verde and Egypt), while Switzerland haven't trailed once all tournament and match up well as a disciplined defensive side, so I nudge the Swiss slightly above the ~18% implied upset price. That plus-money (+450) edge is small and speculative, so I stake only a modest $15 on Switzerland rather than the negative-EV favorite or draw.
refl_key_factor_missed: The attacking-quality gap (Messi and Argentina's forwards) was large enough to break down even a disciplined defense, making a comfortable margin more likely than my 'this will be tight' framing implied.
refl_key_factor_overweighted: Switzerland's 'never trailed all tournament' defensive record, which had been built against weaker attacks and which I used to manufacture a thin value lean.
refl_counterfactual: If Switzerland had scored first and forced Argentina to chase, the game could have been tighter, but Argentina's superior quality still made them likely winners regardless.
refl_would_bet_differently: yes — I'd go no_bet, not because the underdog lost (an ~18% shot loses most of the time) but because the edge I claimed was too thin and narrative-driven to justify staking value.

pre_reasoning: Market consensus across bet365, ESPN and Kalshi puts England (Team B) near 50% in the 90-minute market, the draw ~26%, and Norway ~24%, and I don't see a strong reason to deviate: England carry the deeper squad, the calmer defense (~0.85 xGA/game over their last 10) and big-game penalty reliability, while Norway's route to a win runs heavily through Haaland. The main risk is England's reshuffled back line (Quansah suspended, Reece James/right-back doubts) against the tournament's form striker, which keeps this competitive and 'both teams to score' likely. England is the highest-probability single outcome and some models (e.g. Squawka's 65% to advance) suggest their win price is marginally underrated, so I take a modest stake rather than a large one given the thin pricing edge.
refl_key_factor_missed: none — the 2-1 scoreline matched my modal read almost exactly: England's quality edge decisive, both teams scoring, Norway on the board via their one reliable threat
refl_key_factor_overweighted: England's defensive fragility (Quansah suspension, right-back injuries) — I flagged it as the main risk, but the reshuffled back line conceded only once and held up better than I feared
refl_counterfactual: If Haaland had converted one more of Norway's chances the game likely swings to a draw or extra time, so the outcome was closer to the coin-flip-plus-edge my probabilities implied than the clean result suggests.
refl_would_bet_differently: no — England was the correct and highest-probability side and the modest stake fit a thin pricing edge, though one winning bet doesn't confirm the edge was real

pre_reasoning: The Opta supercomputer and betting markets make France slight favorites in regulation (~41-44% win vs ~29% Spain, ~29% draw), driven by France's 6/6 tournament form and Mbappé's 8 goals, so my probabilities sit close to the efficient market. I take a small-stake value lean on Spain because late-breaking, France-specific injury doubts (Saliba and Upamecano missed Saturday training, Tchouaméni's groin, plus Mbappé's ankle scare) may be underpriced, and Spain—fully fit, defensively elite with one goal conceded all tournament—has won the last two meetings and 7 of the last 10. The edge is thin and Kelly-sized small, hence the low stake rather than a larger commitment.
refl_key_factor_missed: Spain's structural/stylistic dominance in this specific matchup — the Rodri-led double pivot denying France transition moments was something I explicitly identified as the defining battle and then failed to price, and the head-to-head record (7 of last 10, last two meetings) was matchup signal rather than noise.
refl_key_factor_overweighted: France's raw attacking totals (16 goals, Mbappé's 8) and Opta's supercomputer output — the goals were accumulated against Sweden, Paraguay and Morocco, and I anchored my probabilities to the model/market instead of to my own stated reasoning.
refl_counterfactual: Had France's fitness doubts at centre-back and in midfield not materialized, the margin would likely have been narrower, but a 2-0 with Spain's sixth clean sheet in seven points to a stylistic mismatch rather than a coin flip that landed badly.
refl_would_bet_differently: yes — the deeper error was betting Spain while my stated numbers made France the favorite; if I believed the injury and matchup edge enough to stake on it, my probabilities should have said so (roughly Spain 0.38 / draw 0.28 / France 0.34) and the stake should have been larger.

pre_reasoning: Both squads are near full strength (England miss only the suspended Quansah and injured Henderson; Messi recovered from a minor eye issue), so I side with the consensus that England are marginal favourites — deeper squad, neutral indoor Atlanta setting, and an Argentina side that was second-best to Switzerland until a red card, having gone to extra time twice. My 90-minute line of 37/30/33 sits almost exactly on the market (FanDuel England +175 / draw +185 / Argentina +200 implies ~35/33/32 after removing vig; Kalshi 37/34/32; Opta 38.9/27/34.1). The only visible gap is the draw looking a shade rich, which is a fade, not a bet, and England at +175 yields roughly a 1% Kelly stake — well inside my own model error, so no bet is the disciplined call.
refl_key_factor_missed: I treated Argentina's narrow, ride-your-luck knockout wins as evidence of fragility rather than as a repeatable big-game skill — closing out tight matches is precisely what this squad has done for four years, and a one-goal semifinal is exactly the environment where that shows up.
refl_key_factor_overweighted: England's squad depth and the 'neutral indoor Atlanta setting' advantage, plus the Switzerland red-card narrative, which I let discount Argentina more than a 90-minute sample deserved.
refl_counterfactual: In a one-goal game the likeliest single lever is England's makeshift right side — with Quansah available or James fully fit from the start, the margin plausibly flips, though that is a story I am fitting to a scoreline I did not watch.
refl_would_bet_differently: no — I had this at 37/30/33 against a market at roughly 35/33/32, and a single realization of a near-coin-flip reveals no mispricing I could have acted on; no_bet remains the correct decision ex ante.

pre_reasoning: Market prices are near France 50% / draw 25% / England 25% (best available 1.99 / 4.00 / 4.03), and I sit only marginally above that on France: extra recovery day, deeper bench, one England win in the last nine head-to-heads, and a motivation asymmetry (Deschamps' final match in charge plus Mbappé tied with Messi at 8 goals in the Golden Boot race, versus Tuchel already pivoting toward Euro 2028 amid reported camp tension). Offsetting that: Saliba is out, France failed to score for the first time all tournament against Spain, and heavy rotation on both sides makes a bronze final one of the noisiest fixtures to model — so I shade the draw slightly down and keep the stake small. Edge is thin enough that this is close to a no-bet; $15 reflects roughly Kelly-scaled confidence, not conviction.
refl_key_factor_missed: I named the 'bronze finals run loose and high-scoring' thesis but failed to apply it to the winner — with Saliba out and both defenses rotated, England's attack (Kane, Bellingham) was more likely to exploit the chaos than my England-had-weaker-metrics framing allowed.
refl_key_factor_overweighted: France's soft edges — extra recovery day, bench depth, and the Deschamps/Mbappé motivation asymmetry — which mattered little in a 10-goal shootout decided by open, undisciplined defending.
refl_counterfactual: If Saliba had been fit to anchor the French back line, France likely concede fewer than six and the result plausibly flips or draws.
refl_would_bet_differently: yes — no_bet; I already flagged the edge over market as razor-thin in an admittedly noisy fixture, and that combination doesn't justify staking regardless of which side won.

pre_reasoning: Spain's collective control and tournament-best defensive record—one goal conceded in seven matches—give them the edge, while Argentina have needed several difficult knockout-stage escapes despite Messi's eight goals and four assists. The available regulation odds imply roughly 44% Spain, 32% draw and 25% Argentina after removing the bookmaker margin, closely matching my forecast, so I see no meaningful betting value.
refl_key_factor_missed: I underweighted how strongly Spain's defensive control would suppress Argentina's scoring chances.
refl_key_factor_overweighted: Argentina's ability to survive difficult knockout matches and turn a close final in their favor.
refl_counterfactual: If Argentina had converted one of their limited chances, the match could have reached extra time or changed entirely.
refl_would_bet_differently: no, because Spain winning does not show that the pre-match odds offered enough value to justify a bet.
